questions.prompt: Routh-Hurwitz is applied to which polynomial?
questions.explanation: The denominator determines the poles, and the poles determine stability. That is why the Routh-Hurwitz criterion is built from the denominator coefficients of the transfer function.

questions.prompt: Why do we want a method like Routh-Hurwitz?
options: because direct root-solving becomes awkward for higher-order polynomials
because poles no longer matter in stability
because numerators always decide stability first
because quadratic equations have no roots
questions.correctAnswer: because direct root-solving becomes awkward for higher-order polynomials
questions.explanation: For low-order polynomials, solving roots directly is possible. As order increases, that approach becomes inefficient or impractical, so Routh-Hurwitz gives a faster stability test from coefficients alone.

questions.prompt: If all poles are in the left half plane, what is a necessary condition on the coefficients?
options: they must all have the same sign
questions.correctAnswer: they must all have the same sign
questions.explanation: The note shows that stable pole locations imply a same-sign pattern in the denominator coefficients. That makes equal-sign coefficients a necessary condition for stability.

questions.prompt: Why is the equal-sign coefficient condition not sufficient?
options: because a polynomial can still have unstable roots even when all coefficients are positive
because only negative coefficients can be stable
because the numerator cancels the denominator automatically
questions.correctAnswer: because a polynomial can still have unstable roots even when all coefficients are positive
questions.explanation: The note gives a counterexample with all positive coefficients but an unstable quadratic factor. So the sign check is useful as a quick filter, but it cannot guarantee left-half-plane poles by itself.

questions.prompt: Routh-Hurwitz is described in the note as:
options: a necessary and sufficient test for stability
a sufficient but not necessary test
a method for computing the numerator
a shortcut for inverse Laplace transforms
questions.correctAnswer: a necessary and sufficient test for stability
questions.explanation: This is exactly why the method matters. It closes the logical gap left by the simple sign check and tells you whether the denominator poles satisfy the stability requirement.

questions.prompt: What kind of roots is the note trying to rule out when introducing Routh-Hurwitz?
questions.explanation: The stability question is really about whether any denominator roots leave the left half plane. Positive real-part roots are directly associated with unstable behaviour.

questions.prompt: In $H(s)=Z(s)/P(s)$, which part matters for Routh-Hurwitz?
questions.explanation: Routh-Hurwitz tests the pole locations of the system, and those come from the denominator. The numerator influences the response shape, but not the pole-based stability test.

questions.prompt: The main conceptual shift in this lesson is from:
options: explicit roots to coefficient-based stability testing
time domain to frequency domain only
questions.correctAnswer: explicit roots to coefficient-based stability testing
questions.explanation: The previous lesson focused on explicit pole calculation. Routh-Hurwitz keeps the same stability goal but reaches it through the denominator coefficients instead of direct root-solving.

questions.prompt: A polynomial with all positive coefficients is automatically stable:
options: only if it is second order
questions.explanation: That is one of the central warnings in the note. All-positive coefficients are necessary for stability in the cases being studied, but they are not enough to guarantee it.

questions.prompt: What is the practical value of Routh-Hurwitz?
options: it reveals stability directly from coefficients without solving every root
it eliminates the need for denominator polynomials
it turns unstable systems into stable ones
questions.correctAnswer: it reveals stability directly from coefficients without solving every root
questions.explanation: That is the whole point of the method. It makes higher-order stability analysis tractable by replacing direct factorization with a structured coefficient test.
